notes: Dozens of victims, mostly foreign tourists, reported having bank accounts emptied remotely (from Bogotá, Lima, Caracas, the Dominican Republic) shortly after using their card at a bank ATM in Antigua specifically — suspected (unproven) bank-employee complicity, not just street-level skimming. Sourced via CountryReports.org, likely descended from a State Department/OSAC-style report; primary text 403'd on direct fetch, this is a search-indexed summary with that caveat named plainly. The most concretely Antigua-specific of the three crime mechanisms found this pass, and the one held with least confidence given the sourcing chain
"Sweet spot" tier per the file's own three-tier bands
Cleanest desk-findable small-friend-group precedent in either Guatemala location
Vendor's own marketing claim, not independently verified

fact_label: Diligence risk — organized property-fraud ring (Sacatepéquez dept.)
notes: InsightCrime, prosecuted case, 47 arrested — the department Antigua sits in.

fact_label: Distance to Guatemala City / international airport / major hospital
notes: Antigua has decent private clinics for routine care, but anything serious is routed to the capital — a meaningfully shorter buffer than Atitlán's 2.5-3 hours
Against a municipality of ~45,000; some residents dating to the 1980s-90s — several times San Pedro's own foreign-resident ratio
400+ dead nearby, June 2018; Antigua itself sits outside the direct danger zone
TheLatinvestor, triangulated across 3 articles on one site — one voice, not three independent confirmations
Vs. national average 16–20/100,000

National-level mechanism (not Antigua-specific): express kidnapping explicitly named by the UK's gov.uk travel advisory as targeting 'business people and visitors perceived to be wealthy' — short-term opportunistic abductions, victim often picked up via street-hailed taxi, held hours to overnight while criminals force repeated ATM withdrawals. A separate, unverified claim of 12 US citizens ransomed $10,000-150,000 in 2025 could not be confirmed against either candidate embassy page (both returned corrupted content on direct fetch) — named as unverified rather than dropped or trusted. Replaces the prior [GAP] placeholder — a real search was run 2026-07-09 and came back with a real, if partly unverified, answer

Antigua's inland/highland geography (no lake, coast, or border proximity) makes 'outside' the honest call; no single dedicated property.md sentence states this explicitly, so held Medium not High confidence.

A 4.5-year Antigua resident reports a mugging that caused a depressed skull fracture and broken nose — a real severity data point behind the low headline homicide rate, corroborating red-flags.md's "opportunistic theft, not violent crime, but real" framing with an actual incident
Single lived account (Expat Exchange forum) — Guatemala's immigration authority's (IGM's) stated requirements called "only guidelines" by the applicant

value_raw: Hospital Nacional Pedro de Bethancourt (free, national public system) — broad stated scope: general medicine, surgery, gynecology/obstetrics, pediatrics, traumatology/orthopedics, emergency, ICU, diagnostics, rehab/palliative care; mixed reviews (4.0/5 aggregate, real overcrowding/wait-time complaints alongside praise)
notes: locateclinics.com, establecimientosdesalud.mspas.gob.gt (Guatemala's own Ministry of Public Health facility directory), both 2026-07-11; Tripadvisor/Wanderlog reviews are single-pass search synthesis, not cross-verified against a second review platform. Guatemala City's private hospitals (Herrera Llerandi, Centro Médico — modern, US/European-trained staff, 45 min away) are the real capability ceiling beyond this and Antigua's own decent local private clinics
Outskirts (Jocotenango, San Pedro Las Huertas) runs $300–500 instead

A bylined travel writer (WanderTooth) describes Antigua as over-touristed and "a bit vapid" after 15 years of foreign-driven change — a critical read sitting alongside the same countable community-density fact above, not a separate population
35–60% cheaper than the center for comparable space
Resales; new first-sale purchases run higher once VAT included. Atitlán has no separately-documented closing-cost figure of its own.
Dry Dec-Apr, wet May-Oct; no real cold or heat season
Real, current, single-title listing explicitly marketed as flexible for multiple households — concentrated in Panajachel, not San Pedro

notes: A real, materially different emergency-response buffer than Antigua's
Use chartered boats between villages instead — trails not easily reachable by emergency services
One visitor account mentions "regular extortion from locals," unverified beyond that single mention — flagged as something to watch for corroboration, not established fact
2010–2024 warming trend; NASA/USAID runs an active bloom-forecasting dashboard — a monitored, not solved, problem
Confirmed by two independent sources; a government land-reserve lease (OCRET) or S.A. corporation required inside the 200m lakeshore band, direct title outside it.
A real, judge-supervised streamlined route to un-tangle a jointly-purchased parcel later — narrower than a full partition lawsuit

notes: Región V's rate (3.45%) surfaced via secondary press coverage; Región VI's own didn't
Only ~10% of vacation homes/eateries/hostels/luxury stays remain Maya-owned — the sharpest version of a dynamic also visible, less extremely, in San Pedro/San Juan
467.76 m² parcel, ~$47,900 — a sample of one, not a reliable average
Against a total population of ~18,000 — a much smaller absolute and proportional foreign presence than Antigua's
Still the priciest lake town on a relative basis despite the low absolute number — gentrification-driven

One multi-year resident's account (anonymous, unverifiable identity) describes a three-way cultural clash (evangelical/neo-hippie/indigenous Tz'utujil) and a "foreign class" that has "effectively taken control" of the tourism economy — sits alongside two more positive multi-year accounts found in the same file, genuinely contested rather than resolved
Power/wifi cuts specifically May-Oct; site's own source says not sufficient for income-critical remote work needing consistent high speed
livinginguatemala.com table, fills the gap between waterfront and the one inland sample point
Consistent in shape with the $165k-795k waterfront figures found elsewhere in this project's Guatemala research
Same source as above

Hospitalito's own FAQ (directly fetched): Q50 ($6.70) consultations, Q100 ($13.30) emergency visits, Q75-140 ($10-18.70) specialist visits — flat, non-differentiated between foreigners and locals, plus a means-tested sliding-scale discount (25-100%) and a stated no-turn-away-for-inability-to-pay policy. Corrects an earlier uncorroborated "foreigners pay double" claim that had surfaced in initial search results (an AI-generated summary, not independently sourced) — a correction of a worse-sourced inherited claim against the hospital's own primary-source pricing, not a paper-vs-practice enforcement gap, so divergence_flag is left N/A rather than reused for a different kind of correction

value_raw: 24-hour emergency care — the only 24-hour facility within a two-hour radius — plus complete obstetric services, general surgery, and specialty care contingent on which specialist is on hand; 896 surgeries in the most recent reported year, including visiting tertiary specialist teams; private nonprofit, funded by patient fees and donations, not government-funded
notes: hospitalitoatitlan.org/services, hospitalitoatitlan.org/faq (both directly fetched 2026-07-11); the 896-surgeries figure is medangel.org, search-indexed, not independently fetched. San Pedro itself has only a basic public CAP (permanent-care level, not a surgical hospital) plus a smaller Centro de Salud; the full departmental public hospital (Hospital Departamental de Sololá) sits in Sololá town, distance from San Pedro not checked this pass

fact_label: Tenure mechanism cost — government land-reserve lease (OCRET)
notes: Still genuinely unresearched — needs a lawyer/agent conversation.

Lawyers/notaries/developers/brokers become formal "obligated persons"; the informal thin-paper-trail property market won't be a stable assumption past this date. Trajectory qualifier per source: worsening specifically for informal-paper-trail buyers, not the reform overall.

fact_label: Digital Nomad visa converts to permanent residency
notes: Counts toward the 5-year temporary clock like other temporary categories — real on-ramp, ~10 years total to naturalization, slower than Rentista's direct route

Guatemala's immigration authority's own regulation (Acuerdo IGM-016-2025), no averaging permitted. Corrected 2026-07-11: divergence_flag was previously mistagged 'Confirmed-diverges', rendering a false 'written rule doesn't match practice' badge. A 2026-07-10 site-inspection flagged this directly — what was actually found 2026-07-07 is that an inherited claim (a remembered $1,500 figure) was wrong and the regulation's own $2,000 text is correct: a correction to an inherited claim, not a paper-vs-practice enforcement gap. No such divergence exists in the underlying research; corrected to N/A

Source file's own open question: the regulation's text doesn't clearly state which income types qualify — unresolved whether this route accepts passive (non-employment) income cleanly, or leans toward Rentista instead.

Second anchor-verification pass correction: TheLatinvestor's own text names this as applying to Guatemala's most foreigner-popular markets, explicitly including Antigua and Lake Atitlán — a single national finding, not two independently-scoped location facts. Promoted from Antigua/Atitlán duplicate rows (same value, same source, same reasoning at both). Restated near-identically elsewhere in the Atitlán cost-of-living research — a minor "one true source" duplication worth resolving to one home the next time either location's data is touched

fact_label: Public social-security health system (IGSS) closed to Digital Nomad/Rentista foreigners
notes: Guatemala's national social-security health system's (IGSS's) enrollment is employer-triggered only (employer must enroll any worker within 20 days of hire — confirmed directly via livinginguatemala.com's own 2026 IGSS guide); self-employed/freelance/passive-income foreigners fall outside the structure entirely. Both the Digital Nomad and Rentista/Pensionado visas explicitly require no Guatemalan-employer relationship, so IGSS is structurally closed to holders of exactly the routes most likely to be used here, independent of income level. The same finding applies identically at Atitlán

Spot-check finding: a genuine third viable route, distinct from Options 4-6 (work/marriage/tourist), not applicable/not a real plan otherwise. Vehicle most commonly real estate (escritura pública + Registro General de la Propiedad registration); can also be business capital, an earmarked bank deposit, or buying an existing business. Multiple properties can be combined to hit $100k if each is properly documented

fact_label: Investor Visa converts to permanent residency
notes: Converts after 5 years, same as the standard temporary track — NOT the direct-to-permanent shortcut Rentista offers, despite some marketing claims to the contrary. Marketing claims of "citizenship in 9 months" via investment are confirmed false — 5 years continuous residency is required regardless, no investor shortcut

An asset/investment test (verifiable capital placed in a vehicle), not an income test at all — the same shape as Thailand's Long-Term Resident visa (LTR) Wealthy Global Citizen category. Uniquely among Guatemala's routes, this status permits working in one's own business, since it's fundamentally an investment visa rather than a passive-income one.

Seguros G&T, El Roble, BI — scaled by age/coverage. International nomad-insurance floor near $45/month (SafetyWing); Cigna/Allianz/AXA at higher tiers for more comprehensive coverage. Identical figure stated in both `Guatemala/Antigua/overview.md#healthcare` and `Guatemala/Atitlan/overview.md#healthcare` — a genuinely national market, not area-differentiated
The five-shape taxonomy token for Guatemala's government land-reserve (OCRET) band-substitute regime (200m lakeshore, 15km border, 3km coastal, 100m river bands).

value_raw: Same no-titer/no-quarantine framework as dogs, different core-vaccine list
notes: Same paperwork chain as dogs — microchip, rabies vaccination, health certificate, an import permit from Guatemala's agriculture ministry (MAGA), and USDA endorsement — no titer or quarantine either; the only species-specific difference found is the required core-vaccine panel (cats: panleukopenia, rhinotracheitis, calicivirus, leukemia, vs. dogs' distemper/hepatitis/leptospirosis/parvovirus). Replaces the prior [GAP] placeholder — 2026-07-09 research covered both species in the same pass, this just never made it into the export

fact_label: Pet import (dog) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: No rabies titer required; no quarantine if compliant
notes: No rabies titer (FAVN/RNATT) test required regardless of origin country, no quarantine when paperwork is in order — despite Guatemala itself being classified high-rabies-risk by WOAH (the titer requirement concerns origin-country risk classification, not Guatemala's own). Real paperwork still required: ISO 11784/11785 microchip before vaccination, rabies vaccination 21-30 days pre-travel, health certificate within 14 days, core vaccinations (distemper/hepatitis/leptospirosis/parvovirus), parasite treatment, an import permit from Guatemala's agriculture ministry (MAGA), USDA endorsement for US-origin pets. Net: more paperwork steps than a no-vaccine-check destination, but no titer/quarantine puts this toward the easier end of this project's pet-import comparisons. Replaces the prior [GAP] placeholder — this was researched 2026-07-09 but never landed in the export

BMI Seguros' "Ideal" plan states a maximum *new*-enrollment age of 74, with no stated renewal ceiling — could not independently confirm by direct fetch (bmicos.com timed out, 2026-07-11); directionally consistent with `scorecard.md`'s own 13th-criterion framing note (enrollment ceilings "commonly 65-75"). SafetyWing's own ceiling is cited as 64 vs. 69 depending on source consulted — genuinely unresolved, both figures flagged rather than one picked. Real compound gate, not draconian: the Digital Nomad visa's own mandatory-insurance requirement (`Guatemala/visa-legal.md#option-1--digital-nomad-visa-new-launched-oct-2025`) means an applicant outside a nomad-insurer's age ceiling needs a different, likely costlier, locally-issued policy just to qualify for the visa at all. Applies identically at both locations

fact_label: Rentista/Pensionado visa converts to permanent residency
notes: Direct-to-permanent route, skips the temporary-residency wait entirely — the real structural advantage this project keeps cross-checking other candidates against
Stated threshold, no averaging mentioned in source; see financial-fit.md for any specific-income comparison

Pension, rental, investment income, dividends all explicitly qualify; US Social Security named as one of the most commonly accepted sources. This single route already blends pension and ordinary-passive types under one qualifying definition, unlike Colombia's/Argentina's split Rentista-vs-Pensionado routes; a companion pension-detail row would be equally accurate but is not added here (not explicitly instructed).
Also a 15km border strip, 3km coastal band, 100m river band; a government land-reserve lease (OCRET) or S.A. corporation required inside the lakeshore band

notes: Direct title outside the band — Antigua's own confirmed status per the location_applicability row above.

fact_label: President Arévalo — approval rating and impeachment pressure
value_raw: ~35% approval; 13 impeachment petitions and 6 attempts to lift his immunity since taking office (2024); attorney general widely seen as hostile to his reform agenda
notes: Matters more for "will the rules change under me" (visa/tax/property law) than for personal safety day-to-day

value_raw: Mixed record: TSE new magistrates (Mar 2026, "amid corruption fears"); Constitutional Court reappointment of Roberto Molina Barreto (Mar 2026, a real setback for reform); Attorney General replacement of María Consuelo Porras with Rafael García Luna (May 5, 2026, a real institutional win); Comptroller General appointment pending as of Aug 2026
notes: One real win (AG) and one real loss (Constitutional Court) so far — not a verdict either way. Five key oversight/judicial bodies being renamed within a five-month window in 2026 — either a real reform opportunity or a volatile pressure point depending how it goes. Tico Times: Constitutional Court outcome "deals a blow to Arévalo's push for judicial reform", with accusations the US embassy lobbied on the reappointed magistrate's behalf

fact_label: January 2026 national state of emergency / state of siege
value_raw: Declared after 10 police officers killed in coordinated gang attacks; lifted after about a month, but a nationwide "state of prevention" remains in effect
notes: Six months old as of this research, not ancient history. Distinct from the personal-safety/crime axis — named here because a national security-emergency declaration is itself a rules-of-the-game/institutional-capacity signal, not just a crime data point

value_raw: Congress approved the final version June 2, 2026; enters into force ~September 2026; Guatemala's bar association reportedly considering legal challenges
notes: A real, dated example of Guatemala's rules of the game changing on a near-term timeline (property-transaction due-diligence/reporting obligations), not a hypothetical future risk — the property-mechanics detail lives in the land-property-access-tagged row above, this row is the political-process/institutional-change fact
